author: @tim36332
text: Great concept, but I personally wouldn't want to use this setup as a room's heating source. Especially not without a dehumidifier, as the burning of ethanol is going to put a lot of moisture into the room and potentially cause or worsen mould problems. Also, whilst I've not done the sums, Bioethanol on Amazon is currently relatively expensive as a fuel source, at around £7 to £8 per litre on Amazon right now, with one litre burning for around 4 hours. Hence this setup would be quite costly in terms of the fuel price, and it would probably make more sense to just stick on a thermal oil filler radiator.

replies.author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Well, me personally, seeing as I am poor and think like a poor person, would want to put a ceramic lined [this can include crushed up fire brick pieces just laying in the bottom, by the way] fire well in it, and then run it on melted waste plastic.

There's literally tons of it just blowing about, you get it [sort of] 'free' every time you go shopping, it comes as packaging on nearly everything , you really can't get away from the stuff any more, its just all around one!

Just run it through a chopper or shredder

Now this does not mean a leaf shredder, the ones I have seen would do nothing but wrap themselves all up in the plastic, as they are not made for this.

I do not know if a paper shredder can do this, I have never tried, even though I have a couple of old paper shredders here I could try it on, just never occurred to me until just now, but because they move slow and ponderously, I'm kinda thinking they would just bind up.

What you would need is  a really fast spinning motor, with a clean shearing face.

If you know how taffy reacts sort of like a non-Newtonian fluid, this is how one wants to approach shredding plastic.

I have worked with industrial plastic shredders before.

A circle saw with a modified blade set into a box made of builder's board would work, you would not even have to modify the actual saw, in case you could not get a good second hand one, [I have 5 of them because if I see a cheap one at the thrift store for less than 20 bucks, I always pick it up, even if it is not running] but you just need to take the outer cutting edges off of the blade, and, if it is one of those 'ventilated' blades, that is better, because you need to put internal cutting faces on it, just like on an old hand crank style meat grinder, and then you face it up against a good hard steel face, such as even taking a really beat up old file and grinding it smooth and putting a very mild [30 degrees is really all the more you would want on this] cutting edge on it so that it slips JUST by the spinning saw blade [imagine a compound curved tree or brush pruning tool] but at a very high speed, so that the plastic, in essence SHATTERS, more than is 'cut' like a normal pair of scissors.

Doing all of this on a very cold day would make it more efficient, because the colder the plastic is, the more brittle it will be.

Some preliminary cutting would likely be needed, to ensure the plastic fit down the hopper.

Also, you would want to feed the plastic in a little at a time so it did not bind, and so it did not jam and melt.

In an ideal situation, the whole rig would be just freezing cold and you could feed through chips of plastic all day with ease.

The tiny little particles on the output can be  kept in a box or a bag until needed.

Just be sure they are not blown away in the wind as they are produced, or you will be wasting your time and they might even get sucked up into the motor of the saw damaging it. A small bit of coarse cloth, or a scotch brite pad taped over the inlet of the saw cooling vent would be a good idea.

I have a new grinder, and it came from the factory with tiny little air filters built into it.

I had never seen that before, I think its really something all companies should be doing.

replies.author: @pa_maj.MARTINI-van-MAN
replies.text: I'm not sure if you know this Doc but stones from rivers and riverbanks can explode if heated so I'd be careful. If they're not going to be getting too hot they may be fine but it's Good to know this anyway I think. It's the possibility of trapped water expanding to fast to get out causes them to explode. You can check this out for yourself with a Quick google search 👍

author: @torukmahtomahto409
text: To be more efficient, add ceramic wool as absorbent wick for more lasting fire..same as toilet paper alcohol can emergency heater without burning the paper..Search for ethanol burners ceramic wool..

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's not more efficient - if it burns slower it gives out less heat, if you fill the can with wool you have less room for the fuel because half the can is full of wick! so it burns for less time - I am not saying don't add ceramic wall - by all means do - but you are doing that for decorative effect and not efficiency or any kind of improvement beyond looking nice

author: @billunderwood5453
text:
A space heater produces 3.4 BTUs per watt hour.  
Hydrogen contains 1.4 BTUs per liter. 
An HHO generator can produce 35 liters, hence 49 BTUs, per watt hour. (Yes, I'm making an assumption that Brown's Gas is roughly the same BTUs as hydrogen, but I can't find a BTU figure for it.)
Therefore, if you are heating your home with electricity, you could do so for about ¼ the cost by building an HHO generator and burning the Brown’s gas it produces.

replies.author: @marshr32
replies.text: This sounds really interesting, I'm going to look into it! On the surface of it though it almost seems like you're getting something for nothing..... more energy out than you're putting in? Are you able to point me in the right direction to find the numbers for how much hydrogen per kwh etc? Cheers.

replies.author: @marshr32
replies.text: Considering the industrial production of hydrogen, and using current best processes for water electrolysis (PEM or alkaline electrolysis) which have an effective electrical efficiency of 70–80%, producing 1 kg of hydrogen (which has a specific energy of 143 MJ/kg) requires 50–55 kW⋅h (180–200 MJ) of electricity. <<From wikipedia. So it kinda seems like it doesn't work.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: all very true mate - but we are being threatened with blackouts here in the UK - no electricity=no heat if you are relying on that

replies.author: @adamcrofts58
replies.text: What I have been thinking about is an old fashioned idea for heating greenhouses. The principle is that you take a bale of hay and place it in a pit of the same size in the greenhouse. You water it and the heat generated from the decomposition of the hay keeps the greenhouse  warm. So Going with that idea, by placing copper tubes within that bale, attached to a small pump, the tubes then going into a sand battery, would to my way of thinking, once optimised (flow of water etc) give a sustainable heat source. Just a thought. Thanks for your vids, they do spark my mind.

author: @401ksolar
text: Hey I've got an idea, go to the junkyard and look for a Ford V8 that suffered catastrophic failure, remove everything from the cast iron V8 block, add some 4-in stove pipes to the cylinders and place your alcohol burner cans underneath each cylinder, I think that would be a perfect centerpiece for the auto club coffee break room lol

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @l0I0I0I0
text: This comment is slow coming but the reason I wanted to put a venture in the rocket stove heat chamber is so you will not have to put a lid on the vertical fuel feeder tube.  Why?  I don't want to have to fool with feeding wood all day.  You can stick a 10 foot wood pole in the fuel feed in a rocket stove ( not this vids device) and it will slowly burn down sucking air so as to NOT need attending or accidentally catch the long wood fuel on fire.  That's why I wanted a venture at the fuel feed and why it makes sense.  Sorry I didnt elaborate on it at the time of those vids.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am sorry you didn't too lol - but nice share mate and thank you for sharing

author: @ryanlebeck259
text:
That is fantastic. I would have used half of a cinder block because it's chunky and solid set once you've placed it. 

I have also found that to make an oil wick without a piece of pipe you can take the tin from a can lid and cut a slit in it with tin snips about to the center, place your wick, and just fold the can lid in half with pliers.

The can lid heats up the wick oil just as well as the pipe and you already have the lid/can end from making the oil burner anyway.

Love the many ways you have found to do the same thing, but different 😉.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: oh that's a really nice share mate - thanks for taking the time to post it

author: @pdoubleyou7801
text: Shop around for the stove install, I had quotes ranging  £500 to £1500 for installing and hold on to your hat, £750 - £1800 for parts to fit 4 metres of double wall flue.  And it is possible to fit yourself, but then the local building authority has to come and check it.  I did have it fitted by a stove installer in the end, but his quote was good, and I cannot fault his work.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @TheOtherBrook
text: Don't know about over there, but in the US plain cast iron dutch ovens/pots can often be found cheap. Sides and a bottom, some with legs to give a bit of a heat gap. No name 4-5 quart ovens can be as low as $20-$30 new. Or for smaller ones: cast iron sauce pots and soup ramekin. Some of those come with a top with holes that can be used as a trivet or ash catcher. For a fire proof bottom or side screens, I've gotten plain cast iron griddles for as low as $10-$15. Used 3 griddles as heat reflectors for my fireplace. Like everyone here, cheers and thanks for the videos.

replies.author: @delbertwells7318
replies.text: Exactly what I was going to post.  Used and possibly rusty dutch ovens found at thrift stores can be dirt cheap.  This video has inspired me to go look for one, as I know I have seen them in the past.

replies.author: @TheOtherBrook
replies.text: @delbertwells7318 Cast iron disappears FAST from thrift stores around here, I rarely see anything but frying pans, but pots and ovens can often be found in garage sales. Thift Stores seem to have more stainless. I got a big stock pot for $10. Enamel ovens that get chipped are often tossed so you might find one from a friend or family member free. You can't trust food from them. Just be careful if you find one and remove the enamel. It's basically sintered glass. I just saw an 8 qt cast iron oven on the big A online for $20 with free shipping though. Not $5 used cheap but still pretty good price.

replies.author: @Coxeysbodgering
replies.text:
Look for "spirit burner / trangia replacement burner" they are double walled with a row of small holes that the flames come out (I think some non electric hostess trollies / food warmers use them)
Normally they are used in a ventilated inverted pan with another above to hold the cooking pan I've cooked for ours on one and melted through a neighbour's plastic patio table lol

author: @jerrodlopes186
text: I've done one with a paver stone and a small cinder block. It works well. If I had the concrete mold lying around I'd surely try it, but the paver and block cost less than $10 US so it may be a nice inexpensive alternative for people to try.

replies.author: @jerrodlopes186
replies.text: @masterdebater8757 that would certainly work. I've just been in my garage the last 3 hours with my cinder block one burning with a usb powered fan and a solar charged battery bank to run it. I used a tiki torch canister and a carbon felt wick. Works great. The whole thing cost about $50 give or take.

replies.author: @jerrodlopes186
replies.text: @masterdebater8757 awesome!

replies.author: @colfaxschuyler3675
replies.text: @jerrodlopes186 I've seen these heat-powered fans for sitting on wood stoves.  I like the idea, but USB-powered fans are a great idea.

replies.author: @jerrodlopes186
replies.text: @colfaxschuyler3675 I have a heat powered fan. I can tell you from long experience, it doesn't do much to move any heat around. The USB fan will run for days on the 10k mah battery pack without a recharge.

replies.author: @pa_maj.MARTINI-van-MAN
replies.text:
That would only spoil things JT, the fuel disappears very fast from my own experience. Burning Ethanol is like burning paper money, not much heat for value.
It's more a luxury thing or something that replaces a real fire on the ODD occasion that a real fire may be desirable "like setting the mood". In an emergency it could be useful but hey! It's not up to me to tell anyone how they should spend their money 😆

replies.author: @pa_maj.MARTINI-van-MAN
replies.text:
@NewWorldHoarder Same, used now for my camping stoves or lighting other stoves that need to pressurise first.
I now have two paraffin heaters for emergency use, an Aladdin and a Valor; A much better choice for heat than Ethanol Cold Steel 👍They are also collectable and built well, especially the Aladdin. Hey! Tried and tested wins the race this time!
